summary: This paper presents De4D-SLAM, a monocular SLAM framework for dynamic environments that reconstructs the static background and moving objects jointly in a 4D Gaussian Splatting representation, rather than masking dynamic content as outliers. The authors identify what they call an optimization paradox: when static and dynamic Gaussian primitives are optimized together without categorical priors, the more flexible dynamic primitives overfit the static reconstruction residual, collapsing the self-supervised error signal needed to distinguish motion. The main contributions are (i) a gradient-isolated dual-stream design in which a stop-gradient operator prevents the static residual from corrupting the dynamic stream, and those residuals are used to supervise a Spatially-Aware Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (SA-KAN) that predicts a category-agnostic motion probability field; and (ii) a flow-induced initialization that uses dense optical flow to stabilize the non-convex optimization of the 4D primitives. Built on a DROID-SLAM tracking backbone and using external depth (Metric3D) and feature (DINOv2) priors, the method is evaluated on the TUM and Bonn dynamic benchmarks, where it reports competitive camera-tracking accuracy (ATE) and rendering quality (PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS) against dynamic-SLAM baselines, supported by component ablations.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is built around a genuinely important problem and a well-articulated core insight. Rather than discarding moving objects as outliers, which is the dominant strategy in dynamic SLAM, the method reconstructs static and dynamic content jointly, which is the right ambition for embodied perception. The central observation, termed the optimization paradox, is a real and clearly characterized failure mode: when static and dynamic Gaussian primitives are optimized together without categorical priors, the more flexible dynamic primitives overfit the static reconstruction residual and collapse the self-supervised signal that motion discovery depends on. The theoretical account of this capacity mismatch (Eqs. 3-4) is clean, and the proposed remedy, a stop-gradient operator that isolates the dynamic stream from the static residual (Eq. 7), is an elegant and minimal intervention rather than a heavy architectural addition. The flow-induced initialization is similarly well-motivated: it targets a known non-convexity in monocular 4D optimization, is grounded in dense optical flow, and has a complete derivation in the appendix. On the empirical side the results are solid: the method reports the best average ATE on Bonn among holistic-reconstruction methods and competitive rendering quality, and the ablations are unusually thorough, giving per-sequence results that attribute gains to decoupling, SA-KAN, and flow initialization. The qualitative comparisons are convincing, showing that the approach avoids both the holes left by exclusion-based methods and the motion blur produced by naive joint optimization.

That said, several aspects temper my enthusiasm. On originality, the architecture is largely a composition of existing pieces, a DROID-SLAM tracking backbone, 4D Gaussian Splatting, a KAN module, and DINOv2/Metric3D priors, and the paper does not clearly position its decoupling scheme against existing decoupling strategies in dynamic NeRF/SLAM; the genuinely novel contribution is the training-time problem framing and the stop-gradient remedy rather than the network design. The justification for SA-KAN is the weakest part of the method. It is not established that a Kolmogorov-Arnold layer is the right choice here: the parameter-efficiency argument (O(N^2 G) versus O(N^2)) is misleading, since a KAN layer in fact has more parameters per layer than a comparable MLP by roughly the grid factor G, and the efficiency reported comes from using a smaller network, not from any intrinsic property of KANs. The measured advantage over an MLP is marginal (on the order of 0.06 cm ATE), while the latency cost is substantial (roughly 2.57 ms versus 0.30 ms per call), and no comparison against a spatially-augmented MLP, the more appropriate baseline, is provided. The motion model also sits in tension with the paper's claims: the dynamic representation is a linear velocity model, yet the paper repeatedly claims to capture arbitrary non-rigid motion. The argument that temporal opacity windowing yields a piecewise-linear approximation of curved trajectories is under-analyzed, with no error bound, no control over how the learned temporal extent interacts with approximation quality, and no experiment on genuinely complex deformations such as articulated limbs, rotating objects, or fluid-like motion.

The experimental scope is the other main limitation. Evaluation is confined to two small-scale indoor RGB-D benchmarks (TUM and Bonn) with relatively simple dynamics; there is no test on outdoor or driving datasets or on scenes with diverse object categories, which leaves the generality of the approach unproven. For a system paper the efficiency reporting is also incomplete: the method does not run in real time, but end-to-end tracking and mapping FPS are not reported, and there is no per-submodule timing breakdown that would let a reader identify the bottlenecks. Because motion segmentation via SA-KAN is a central contribution, I would also expect a quantitative evaluation of mask accuracy rather than qualitative figures alone, since tracking ATE does not by itself validate mask precision. The baseline comparison would benefit from more transparency, because the input modalities of the baselines (monocular versus RGB-D, e.g., SplaTAM, DynaSLAM) are not stated explicitly, and some reported margins, for instance over WildGS in Tables 1 and 3, are small enough that the accompanying text reads as stronger than the numbers support. Finally, the ablations do not isolate the contribution of the Metric3D depth prior or the DINOv2 features, and a few presentation issues remain: Figure 2 is ambiguous in places (no explicit component for distinguishing static from dynamic pixels, the DBA block placed outside the mapping module, and a 'next frame' input that could be misread as using future frames), and the lack of supplementary video makes temporal consistency and motion quality hard to judge.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Have you compared SA-KAN against a spatially-augmented MLP (rather than a standard MLP), and can you justify the choice of a KAN given that the measured ATE improvement is marginal while the per-call latency is roughly an order of magnitude higher?

2. What is the end-to-end tracking and mapping throughput (FPS), and can you provide a per-keyframe, per-submodule timing breakdown (DINOv2 feature extraction, SA-KAN, flow lifting, 4D Gaussian optimization) so that the bottlenecks to real-time operation can be identified?

3. How much of the improvement is attributable to the decoupling strategy and dual-stream architecture versus the Metric3D depth prior and DINOv2 features? An ablation isolating these external priors would clarify the source of the gains.

4. How does the linear velocity model with temporal opacity windowing handle genuinely complex non-rigid motion? Is there an approximation-error or primitive-count analysis for fast, articulated, or deformable objects, and how sensitive is the method to fast object motion and to the quality of the input optical flow?

5. Can you provide a quantitative evaluation of the SA-KAN motion-segmentation accuracy (beyond qualitative masks), clarify the input modalities of all baselines in Tables 1-3 (monocular versus RGB-D), and report the sensitivity of the results to the static/dynamic threshold tau?
limitations: The limitations discussion is largely adequate, as the authors openly acknowledge that the system does not operate in real time. It would be strengthened by also addressing the restricted evaluation scope (small indoor RGB-D scenes only) and the reliance on external depth, feature, and optical-flow priors, since both bear directly on the generality of the claims.
